FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data; n=1,415 participants at baseline (ages 9-10)

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures passive sensing smartphone use via ABCD-EARS application: keyboard use, app use, passive sensing data; text: 'ABCD-EARS application recorded (1) the active application in the foreground of the participant's phone, (2) the application's App Store/Play Store category, (3) the number of keystrokes input'

FT_IC3.evidence: Only validation study comparing passive measures (keyboard use, app use) to self-report correlations; no association test to non-SM outcome. Reports: 'Keyboard and app-use measures were reliable and positively correlated with one another (r=0.33) and with self-reported use (rs=0.21-0.35)' but these are screen-to-screen validation, not SM→non-SM associations

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports correlation coefficients: r=0.33 (keyboard-app), rs=0.21-0.35 (self-report concordance)

FT_IC5.evidence: Measurement and validation paper only; reports passively-sensed smartphone use measures and their reliability/concordance with self-report, but does not report association between smartphone/SM variable and a non-SM outcome

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in Scientific Reports 2024

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails FT_IC3 and FT_IC5; FT_EC7 only applies to papers that pass all inclusion criteria
notes: This is a measurement paper that validates EARS passive sensing against self-report and describes smartphone use trends. E5 applies: measurement paper without external outcome analysis (only measurement concordance) = NOT_MET for FT_IC5.

FT_IC1.evidence: Discusses ABCD Study: 'The Adolescent Brain and Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, involving over 11,000 youth and their families, is a groundbreaking project'

FT_IC2.evidence: This is an infrastructure/resource paper describing the NowIKnowMyABCD website and discussion board. Does not measure or report on any specific smartphone or social media variable. Paper focuses on centralized documentation and communication resource platform, not on SM/phone data analysis.

FT_IC3.evidence: No statistical tests or associations reported. This is a resource description paper with no empirical analysis of SM/phone variables.

FT_IC4.evidence: No numeric values (β, OR, r, p) reported for any SM/phone associations

FT_IC5.evidence: Not an empirical analysis. This is a resource/infrastructure paper describing the development and features of a centralized help platform (website and discussion board) for ABCD researchers. E10 applies: no original analysis present.

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in Developmental Cognitive Neuroscience 2024

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails FT_IC2, FT_IC3, and FT_IC5; does not qualify for FT_EC7 evaluation
notes: E10 applies: special-issue/resource paper with no original empirical analysis = NOT_MET. This is purely a methods/infrastructure paper describing a community resource platform.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data: 'N = 11,868 participants in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study followed from ages ~9/10 to ~13/14'

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures multiple specific technology subtypes: social media, texting, video chat, YouTube, TV/movies, video games. Text: 'youths' self-reported hours per day (hr/day) of technology subtypes: TV/Movies, video games, YouTube, social media, video chat, and texting'

FT_IC3.evidence: Multilevel logistic regression examining associations between technology subtypes and sociodemographic variables (sex, parent education, income, marital status). Both cross-sectional and longitudinal analyses. E.g., 'Using child-reported hours of technology use, increases in the subject-specific odds of using a technology >2 hr/day ranged from 25% (YouTube; 95% CI: 1.16-1.35) to 234% (social media; 95% CI: 3.14-3.55)'

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports OR with 95% CI for multiple associations: social media OR 3.34 (95% CI: 3.14, 3.55); TV OR 2.16 (95% CI: 2.09, 2.23); texting OR 2.22 (95% CI: 2.07, 2.39); video chat OR 1.79 (95% CI: 1.67, 1.93); YouTube OR 1.25 (95% CI: 1.16, 1.35); video games OR 2.11 (95% CI: 2.04, 2.18). Table 2 provides extensive ORs with CIs.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: 'We used multilevel logistic regression to examine participant-specific changes and between-subject heterogeneity in the probability of engaging with each technology for more than two hours per day' with longitudinal data across 5 waves

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in Journal of Adolescent Health 2024

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes cross-sectional analyses with sociodemographic predictors of technology use (FT_EC7 condition (a) met for some analyses), but also includes cross-sectional data with multiple predictor types and technology use as both DV and IV in different contexts. E14 applies: sociodemographic predictors of phone/SM use with cross-sectional analysis = MET (keep INCLUDE). Cross-sectional analyses present; FT_EC7 does not apply.
notes: E7 and E14 apply: technology use as outcome with sociodemographic predictors; cross-sectional analyses are present, so FT_EC7 does not apply (cross-sectional ignores directionality). Paper is INCLUDE.
